coredata.dc:title: Insight into the corrosion inhibition performance of Jasmine flower extract on copper in sulfuric acid medium using experimental and theoretical calculation methods
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Tengteng
Tan, Bochuan
Liu, Jia
Chen, Jida
Wei, Hao
Zhang, Fan
Al-Zaqri, Nabil
Li, Wenpo
coredata.dc:description:
Background Copper is widely used in all areas of society, but it is easy to be corroded in corrosive media, which causes certain economic losses. Among many anti-corrosion methods, adding corrosion inhibitors is an efficient, convenient and common method. However, there are some shortcomings in traditional corrosion inhibitors, so it is urgent to develop an efficient corrosion inhibitor with no toxicity and low cost as well as no harm to the environment.
Methods The corrosion performance of Jasmine flower extract (JFE) for copper in 0.5 mol/L sulfuric acid medium was studied by electrochemical test, and the microstructure and chemical composition of copper surface were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscope (SEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM). The interaction mechanism between corrosion inhibitor molecules and copper was analyzed based on theoretical calculation.
Significant findings The results show that JFE is an efficient and environment-friendly corrosion inhibitor. In a certain temperature range (298 ∼ 308 K), when the concentration of JFE is 500 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) is not less than 94.0%. According to Langmuir adsorption model, the JFE adsorbs on the copper surface as a mixed type of inhibitor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper
Jasmine flower extract
Corrosion inhibitor
Theoretical calculation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number ( RSP2023R396 ), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, Municipal Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (no. CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1189 and CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1369 ), Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST ( 2022QNRC001 ), Opening Project of Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (no. 2021CL30 ), Research Foundation of Chongqing University of Science and Technology (no. ckrc2021052 ), Opening Project of Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province ( 2022CL18 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nyquist (a) and Bode (b) diagrams of copper soaked in different concentrations of JFE at 298 K.
Nyquist (a, c) and Bode (b, d) diagrams of copper soaked in blank solution (a, b) and 500 mg/L JFE (c, d) at different temperatures.

Open circuit curves (a, c, e), Polarization curves (b, d, f) of copper soaked in 0.5 mol/L sulfuric acid solution containing different concentrations of JFE at 298 K (a, b), without (c, d) and with (e, f) 500 mg/L JFE at different temperatures.
ATR-FTIR spectrum of (a) JFE, (b) copper sheet soaked in JFE.
XPS diagram of copper soaked in 500 mg/L JFE: (a) Survey, (b) Cu 2p, (c) C 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) N 1s.
Langmuir adsorption curve of EIS data at 298K.
SEM images of copper sheets soaked in blank solution (a, b, c) and 500 mg/L JFE (d, e, f) at different temperatures.

Two-dimensional and three-dimensional of copper in various conditions: images of newly polished copper (a, b); images of copper soaked in blank solution (c, d) and 500 mg/L JFE (e, f) at 298 K for 14 hours.
Structure, HOMO, LUMO and ESP maps of quercetin, kaempferol, berberine and salicifoline.
Side (a, c, e, g) and top (b, d, f, h) views of the optimized adsorption configurations of quercetin, kaempferol, berberine and salicifoline on Cu (111) surface.
Adsorption mechanism of JFE on copper interface.
Fitting data of EIS of copper soaked in different concentrations of JFE at distinct temperatures.
Polarization curve fitting parameters of copper under different conditions.
Quantum Chemical Correspondence Data of Quercetin, Kaempferol, Berberine and salicifoline.

head.ce:title: Insight into the corrosion inhibition performance of Jasmine flower extract on copper in sulfuric acid medium using experimental and theoretical calculation methods

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Metallurgy and Materials Engineering
Chongqing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Engineering, Chongqing Chemical Industry Vocation College, Chongqing 400020, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering and Design, School of Engineering and Informatics, University of Sussex, Brighton BN1 9RH, United Kingdom
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eDepartment of Engineering and Design, School of Engineering and Informatics, University of Sussex, Brighton BN1 9RH, United Kingdom

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2455, Riyadh, 11451, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: fDepartment of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2455, Riyadh, 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper is widely used in all areas of society, but it is easy to be corroded in corrosive media, which causes certain economic losses. Among many anti-corrosion methods, adding corrosion inhibitors is an efficient, convenient and common method. However, there are some shortcomings in traditional corrosion inhibitors, so it is urgent to develop an efficient corrosion inhibitor with no toxicity and low cost as well as no harm to the environment.

The corrosion performance of Jasmine flower extract (JFE) for copper in 0.5 mol/L sulfuric acid medium was studied by electrochemical test, and the microstructure and chemical composition of copper surface were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscope (SEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM). The interaction mechanism between corrosion inhibitor molecules and copper was analyzed based on theoretical calculation.

The results show that JFE is an efficient and environment-friendly corrosion inhibitor. In a certain temperature range (298 ∼ 308 K), when the concentration of JFE is 500 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) is not less than 94.0%. According to Langmuir adsorption model, the JFE adsorbs on the copper surface as a mixed type of inhibitor.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper has excellent thermal conductivity, electrical conductivity, ductility, machinability, and weldability, so it is widely used in electrical, electronic, artistic, chemical, electric power, transportation, aerospace and other industries [1–8]. However, in humid environment, copper is prone to oxidation and corrosion. The corrosion of copper not only affects its performance, but also causes economic losses and potential safety hazards. Acidic solution can be used to clean the corrosion products on the copper interface, and sulfuric acid is one of the frequently-used pickling agents. Although sulfuric acid can remove the oxidized substances on the copper surface, it will damage the copper matrix. In order to protect copper from acid corrosion, it is pivotal to mix a proper amount of efficient corrosion inhibitor into sulfuric acid.

It is well known that there are two kinds of common corrosion inhibitors in the market: inorganic and organic corrosion inhibitors. Among them, inorganic corrosion inhibitors generally include chromate, phosphate and molybdate, while organic corrosion inhibitors are mainly organic compounds containing phosphorus, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, aromatic rings and heterocyclic rings [6,9–21]. Although these inhibitors are effective in inhibiting metal corrosion, there are still some shortages, such as large dosage, high price and harm to the environment, which limit their development and application prospects. Thus, it is particularly important to find efficient, environmentally friendly, low cost and no toxicity corrosion inhibitor to replace traditional corrosion inhibitor. Plant extracts are considered as green and sustainable materials because of their wide sources, low cost, no toxicity and no pollution, which have attracted the attention of researchers. Over the years, it has been reported that various plant extracts such as Garlic, Epimedium, Saccharum officinarum leaf and Acalypha torta leaf are potential corrosion inhibitors [22–25]. However, the extraction solvents used in the above literatures are all organic solvents, and the extraction process is very complicated. Therefore, it is meaningful to find a simple extraction procedure with environmental-friendly extraction solvents for obtaining an efficient corrosion inhibitor from plants.

In this experiment, Jasmine flower was extracted with water. Compared with organic solutions, water as a solvent has many advantages. For example, the extraction procedure is simple and environmental-friendly, and the extraction solvent is available, cheap, nontoxic, non-flammable and stable. Jasmine flower has a wide source and is widely planted in Jiangsu, Guangxi, Fujian and Guangdong in China. Besides, Jasmine flower is not only a Chinese traditional medicine but also materials of Chinese tea, and has been demonstrated to be good for health. According to literature reported, Jasmine flower extract contains a lot of flavonoids and phenolic compounds [26,27], which make Jasmine flower extract have the potential to become an excellent corrosion inhibitor. However, research on Jasmine flower extract as a corrosion inhibitor has not been publicly reported. Therefore, the corrosion inhibition of Jasmine flower extract for copper in 0.5 mol/L sulfuric acid solution was studied. The corrosion inhibition efficiency was tested by electrochemistry experiment, the bonding information was analyzed by FTIR and XPS, and the morphology was observed by SEM and AFM. In addition, the adsorption on the copper interface was analyzed by theoretical calculation. Combined with experiments and theories, the corrosion inhibition ability of JFE components is analyzed, which provides a reliable theoretical basis for the selection of green corrosion inhibitors in the future.

During the flowering period in summer, Jasmine flowers were picked on sunny days, dried in the sun and ground into powder to obtain Jasmine flower powder. Jasmine flower powder used in this experiment was bought in an ordinary shop in Heng County, Guangxi Province. First, 50 g Jasmine powder and 2 L ultrapure water were added into a container, and heated at 80 ℃ on a stove for 6 hours. Then, the mixture was filtered with a filter cloth while it was hot, and the extract was concentrated again to about 20 mL. The obtained concentrate was pre-frozen in the refrigerator for one day, and then freeze-dried for two days. Finally, yellow powdery solid was collected and stored for the experiment. High Performance Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometer (LCMS-8060) was used to detect the main components of the Jasmine flower extract.

A copper cube (1 cm x 1 cm x 1 cm) was welded with a copper wire with a length of about 10 cm on the surface and sealed with epoxy resin for making a working electrode. The electrode area that was exposed to corrosive environment was 1 cm2. Before testing, the working electrode was polished with abrasive paper (400, 800, 1200, 2000 mesh) until a smooth and bright surface was obtained. Then, the surface of the working electrode was cleaned with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, and dried with a blower.

In the test, the blank control solution was 0.5 mol/L dilute sulfuric acid solution made of concentrated sulfuric acid (98% by weight) and deionized water. The experimental group was blank solution with different concentrations of JFE (50, 100, 300, 500 mg/L). It should be noted that a new test solution was used at the beginning of each measurement.

The electrochemical experiment of the three-electrode system was carried out with electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E). The three-electrode system was composed of a working electrode (copper), reference electrode (saturated calomel electrode) and the counter electrode (platinum electrode). All tests were carried out in a constant-temperature water bath, and the tests were conducted at three temperatures (298, 303 and 308 K). First, the open circuit potential (OCP) test was conducted for 30 minutes to obtain a stable open circuit potential. Then, in the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test, the disturbance signal was a 10−2 V sine wave in the frequency range from 105 to 10−2 Hz. Finally, Tafel polarization curve was tested based on EOCP value. The selected potential interval was ± 0.25 V, and the scanning rate was 10−3 V/s. To meet the repeatability, all tests were repeated three times in the same conditions.

Before testing, the copper samples were soaked in blank solution and 500 mg/L JFE. The FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet iS50) was used to detect the functional groups of JFE and JFE adsorbed on copper interface and XPS (PHI 5700) was used to study the adsorption mechanism of JFE in Al-Ka X-ray source. In addition, SEM (JEOL-JSM-7800F) and AFM (MFP-3D-BIO) were used to observe the two-dimensional and three-dimensional corrosion morphology.

In Gaussion 09 program, according to B3LYP method in density functional theory (DFT), the structure of the inhibitor molecule under study was optimized and calculated by using keywords (opt freq) at the level of 6-311++G(d, p) basis set. The occupied molecular orbit (HOMO) with the highest energy and the unobccupied molecular orbit (LUMO) with the lowest energy were calculated, and the dipole moment (μ) was obtained.

Materials Studio2019 software was used to simulate the force of inhibiting molecules on Cu (111) surface. Cu (111) surface was chosen because of its dense packing and low miller indices, so it can replace copper surface. First, a 3D Cu (111) model (6×6×6) was constructed, the dimension of the whole model was 15 Å × 20 Å × 50 Å and the vacuum layer (20 Å) was filled with 300 H2O molecules and a corrosion inhibitor molecule. In the dynamic calculation, Force field was COMPASS, the simulated temperature and ensemble were 298 K and NVT. Besides, time step and total simulation time were 1 fs and 600 ps, respectively.

According to literature reports [28,29], Jasmine flower extract contains many chemicals, among which quercetin, kaempferol, berberine and salicifoline may have corrosion inhibition ability. Structural formulas of quercetin, kaempferol, berberine and salicifoline are as shown in Fig. 1 . Moreover, the existence of these four substances in JFE is confirmed by HPLC-MS, and the results are shown in Figs. S1 and S2.

The EIS test was carried out to quickly obtain kinetic and surface structure information from the surface of the working electrode. The AC impedance method was used to study the effect of different temperatures and distinct contents of JFE on the corrosion inhibition ability of copper in sulfuric acid.

In Fig. 2 (a), the Nyquist diagram of the blank solution consists of an irregular arc and a direct line. The irregular arc in the high frequency region is relevant to double layer capacitance and charge transfer resistance, while the straight line in the low frequency region matches Warburg impedance, which is relevant the diffusion of corrosion products and dissolved oxygen. Additionally, the radius of capacitive reactance arc obviously enlarges with the increase of JFE concentration. This shows that JFE can be adsorbed at the interface of copper, increasing the resistance of charge transfer, thus achieving the effect of inhibiting copper corrosion. Fig. 2(b) shows that when JFE concentration increases, the impedance modulus map becomes higher and the phase angle plot becomes wider, which illustrate that JFE can be adsorbed on the copper surface and have good corrosion inhibition performance [30,31]. Furthermore, In Fig. 3 , when the temperature rises, the capacitive reactance radius decreases, and the impedance diagram and phase angle diagram become lower and narrower, which shows the rise of temperature can aggravate the corrosion of copper.

The equivalent circuit in Fig. 4 was used to fit the original impedance data, and the fitted data were listed in Table 1 , including solution resistance (Rs ), film resistance (Rf ), charge transfer resistance (Rct ), electric double layer capacitance (Cdl ), Warburg impedance (W), constant phase angle elements (CPE), film capacitance (Cf ) and standard deviation (SD). The impedance of CPE can be obtained by formula (1) [32–37].
(1)
Z
C P E
=
1
Y 0
(
j ω
)
n

Where Y0 represents CPE constant, j and ω refer to imaginary unit and angular frequency, n refers to the deviation index of the surface inhomogeneity of the reaction electrode, and its value is between −1 and 1. It is reported that CPE is equivalent to inductance, pure resistance, Warburg impedance and pure capacitance, and the corresponding values of n are −1, 0, 0.5 and 1, respectively [31].

The value of C can be obtained by formula (2) [38–41]:
(2)
C =
Y 0
( ω )
n − 1
=
Y 0
(
2 π
f
z i m − M a x
)
n − 1
Where f
zim − Max represents the frequency matched by the largest imaginary part of impedance. The η is obtained by formula (4) [42–45].
(3)
R p
=
R f
+
R
c t
(4)
η % =
R p
−
R
p , o
R p
× 100
Where Rp and Rp,0 refer to the total impedance of the copper electrode in sulfuric acid containing different concentrations of JFE and without JFE respectively.

Table 1 indicates that the Rct value increases with the increase of JFE concentration. Therefore, it is inferred that an adsorption film is formed on the surface of copper, thus inhibiting the charge transfer process. Moreover, it is obviously that, with the increase of JFE concentration, Cdl and Cf decrease, which indicates that with the increase of JFE concentration, a large number of inhibitory molecules can replace the position and adsorption of H2O molecules at the copper interface, thereby forming a dense adsorption film to protect the copper interface. As a result, the η increases with the increase of JFE content, reaching 94.0% at 298 K when JFE concentration is 500 mg/L. Besides, it is obvious that Rct of blank solution decreases with the rise of temperature, which indicates that the corrosion of copper is aggravated by the increase of temperature. However, at 308 K, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of JFE remains above 90%, which indicates that JFE is an effective inhibitor and can maintain excellent corrosion inhibition in a certain temperature range.

Fig. 5 (a, c, e) shows the open circuit curves of copper in different conditions. The EOCP value is stable, which means that the adsorption and desorption state at the interface of the working electrode has reached a dynamic equilibrium. Fig. 5(b, d, f) shows the polarization curves of copper under different conditions. According to reports, the corrosion mechanism of copper in blank solution is as follows:

Anode reaction:
(5)
Cu → C
u
a d s
+
+
e −
(6)
C
u
a d s
+
→ C
u
s o l
2 +
+
e −

Cathode reaction:
(7)
1 2
O 2
+ 2
H +
+ 2
e −
→
H 2
O

According to formulas (3) and (4), it was found that copper is easy to react in sulfuric acid solution to form
C
u
a d s
+
and
C
u
s o l
2 +
. The formula (6) is the rate-determining step.

In Fig. 5(b), when the content of JFE is increased at 298 K, the corrosion potential becomes more negative, which is one of the characteristics of cathode corrosion inhibitors. In addition, with the increase of JFE content, the anode current density and cathode current density decrease, but the cathode current density decreases more, which illustrates that JFE can hinder the dissolution of copper ions in the anode and hydrogen evolution in the cathode, but mainly affects the cathode reaction.

As shown in Fig. 5(d), when the temperature is raised, the value of corrosion current density (i corr) increases, indicating that the temperature can accelerate the erosion of copper in sulfuric acid solution without JFE. This is in agreement with the conclusion of the impedance experiment. The i corr and the slope data of anode and cathode are listed in Table 2 . Wherein, the η can be calculated by formula (8) [17,19,35,46,47]:
(8)
η % =
i
c o r r , 0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r , 0
× 100
In which icorr and icorr,0 are the current densities of working electrodes in 0.5 mol/L sulfuric acid containing different concentrations of JFE and without JFE respectively.

Besides, the corrosion rate (R corr) of copper in sulfuric acid with and without JFE can be calculated by Formula (9):
(9)
R
c o r r
=
0.13 ×
i
c o r r
×
M r
d
Where M r is on behalf of the relative atomic mass of copper and d is its density. The values of R corr are listed in Table 2.

As shown in Table 2, with the increase of JFE content, the absolute value of Tafel slope (βc ) of cathode decreases steadily, indicating that the mechanism of cathode has changed. This may be due to the adsorption of inhibitor on copper interface and the thickening of electric double layer. The values of the anode Tafel slope with all the additions of JFE are higher than the values without the addition, which may be due to the adsorption of sulfate ions or inhibitor molecules on the copper surface [48,49].

According to the literature, when the difference of corrosion potential between the solution containing inhibitor and the blank solution exceeds 85 mV, the inhibitor is an anode or cathode inhibitor, otherwise, it is a mixed inhibitor [50,51]. In this study, when the concentration of JFE is 50, 100, 300 and 500 mg/L, compared with the blank solution, the deviation values of corrosion potential are 70, 78, 90 and 109 mg/L, respectively. Therefore, it can be concluded that at lower concentration (50, 100 mg/L), JFE acts as a mixed corrosion inhibitor and mainly affects the cathode, while at higher concentration (300, 500 mg/L), it mainly acts as a cathode corrosion inhibitor.

The FTIR diagram of JFE is shown in Fig. 6 (a). It clearly shows that there are many functional groups in JFE. These peaks with wave numbers of 3247 and 1376 cm−1 are O-H, and there are other peaks C-H (2934 and1450 cm−1), C=C and C=N (1620 cm−1). Peaks with wave numbers of 1081 and 1022 cm−1 are matched with C-N and C-O. In addition, those peaks with wave numbers below 1000 cm−1 involve aromatic and aliphatic C-H groups [52,53]. FTIR test shows that JFE contains many functional groups (O-H, C=C, C=N, C-N, C-O), which make JFE adsorbed on copper interface.

Fig. 6(b) shows the FTIR spectrum of the copper sheet soaked in JFE. The wave number of 3087cm−1 is attributed to O-H stretching. The wave number in 1649 cm−1 is connected with C=C and C=N stretching [54]. The wave number of 1048 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching of C-N and C-O [55]. FTIR test shows that JFE can be adsorbed on the surface of copper.

For further study of the bonding information between copper and JFE, the XPS test was carried out. Copper sheet was soaked in sulfuric acid solution with 500 mg/L JFE at 298 K for 14 hours. The measured spectrums are shown in Fig. 7 . The presence of copper, carbon, oxygen and nitrogen on the copper sheet can be clearly found. The peaks of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 are shown in Fig 7(b), and their binding energies are 932.3 and 951.9 eV, respectively [56].

In addition, the peak of copper ion can be found, but the peak is weak, indicating that JFE can hinder the deep oxidation of copper. In Fig. 7(c), There are four obvious peaks at the binding energies of 284.8, 285.3, 286.5 and 288.6 eV in the XPS spectrum of C 1s, which correspond to C-C, C=N, C-N and C-O respectively [57]. In Fig. 7(d), two peaks of CuO/Cu2O and C-O-C can be clearly found in the XPS spectrum of O 1s, and their binding energies are 531.1 and 531.9 eV, respectively [58].

As shown in Fig. 7(e), there are three characteristic peaks at the binding energies of 399.2 eV, 399.8 and 400.3eV, which are N=C, N-C and N-Cu bonds, respectively. The results show that JFE can form N-Cu bond with copper, and then adsorb on the interface of copper.

In this study, Langmuir adsorption isotherm was used to analyze the adsorption behavior. The expression of Langmuir is shown in Formula (10) [44,59]:
(10)
1 − θ
θ
=
K
a d s
C

Where θ represents surface coverage, K ads refers to adsorption equilibrium constant, and C represents the concentration of JFE.
Δ
G
a d s
0
(the standard adsorption free energy) can be obtained by formula (11) [41,45,60,61]:
(11)
K
a d s
=
1
55.5
exp
(
−
Δ
G
a d s
0
R T
)
R in the formula refers to the ideal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), 55.5 represents the concentration of water in moles, and T represents the absolute temperature.

In Fig. 8 , the value of
Δ
G
a d s
0
is negative, indicating that JFE has the ability to spontaneously and tightly adsorb at the copper/solution interface. The value of
Δ
G
a d s
0
is reported to be relevant to the adsorption types. If
Δ
G
a d s
0
is greater than −20 kJ/mol, it means that there is electrostatic interaction between corrosion inhibitor and metal, resulting in physical adsorption. When the value of
Δ
G
a d s
0
is close to or less than −40 kJ/mol, it indicates that there is charge transfer or sharing between corrosion inhibitor molecules and metal interface to form chemical bonds, which is chemical adsorption. The value of
Δ
G
a d s
0
is between −40 and −20 kJ/mol, that is, there is physical adsorption and chemical adsorption [60,62]. The results shows that ΔG0 ads =-27.61 kJ/mol, which indicates that the JFE at the copper/solution interface belongs to physical and chemical adsorption at 298 K.

It is shown in Fig. 9 (a, b, c), the surface of copper sheets soaked in the blank solution are rough and severely corroded. Moreover, with the rise of temperature, the corrosion situation is aggravated. However, the interface of copper sheets immersed in 500 mg/L JFE solution are relatively flat from Fig. 9(d, e, f), which shows that JFE can hinder the corrosion of copper interface.

Fig. 10 shows two-dimensional and three-dimensional images of copper under different conditions. In Fig. 10(a, b), the interface of the newly polished copper sheet is almost flat and smooth, and the average roughness value (Ra) is 6.3 nm. However, Fig. 10(c, d) show that the interface of copper soaked in blank solution is severely corroded and very rough, and the Ra is 98.2 nm. While the interface of copper immersed in JFE is smooth and flat and the value of Ra is 29.9 nm, as shown in Fig. 10(e, f). Therefore, the existence of JFE can hinder the corrosion of copper.

In this study, quantum computation was used to study the relationship between the η of organic molecules and their structure [63]. In Fig. 11 , there are the structure, electron cloud distribution of frontier orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) and electrostatic potential (ESP) maps of quercetin, kaempferol, berberine and salicifoline. Fig. 11 show that the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of quercetin, kaempferol, berberine and salicifoline are uniformly distributed on benzene ring and six-membered ring. It can be speculated that these four molecules are adsorbed on the interface of copper, thus obtaining a larger coverage to protect copper. Moreover, the ESP maps consist of red and blue regions related to nucleophilic activity and electrophilic activity, respectively. The red area is distributed on benzene ring, O and N atoms, and these groups can form coordination bonds with copper atoms.

In addition, the related data are shown in Table 3 , including E HOMO, E LUMO, energy gap (ΔE = E LUMO – E HOMO) [64] and dipole moment (μ) [65]. Other parameters such as global hardness (η), electronegativity (χ) and the number of transferred electrons (ΔN) can be obtained according to the following equation [66]:
(12)
χ = −
E
H O M O
+
E
L U M O
2
(13)
η =
E
L U M O
−
E
H O M O
2
(14)
Δ
N =
χ
C u
−
χ
i n h
2
(
η
C u
+
η
i n h
)
χ Cu, χ inh, ηCu and ηinh represent electronegativity and chemical hardness of copper and inhibitor molecules, respectively. In which χ Cu and η Cu are equal to 4.48 and 0 eV mol−1, respectively.

In the Table 3. It can be seen that ΔN of quercetin, kaempferol, berberine and salicifoline are positive (0.10668, 0.11108, 0.08407 and 0.22836, respectively), indicating that electrons can be transferred from the inhibitor molecule to the copper interface, so JFE has very high corrosion inhibition efficiency. It is reported that low energy gap and dipole distance are the main reasons to improve the suppression efficiency. Therefore, the order of inhibitory effect of four molecules is : berberine>kaempferol>quercetin>salicifoline.

It can be clearly observed from the Fig. 12 , quercetin, kaempferol, berberine run parallel to the copper interface nearly, and this orientation can reduce the touch between corrosive substances and the copper interface to protect the copper from being corroded.

The interaction energy between the four substances and the copper matrix can be obtained by formula (15) [36,67]:
(15)
E
i n t e r a c t
=
E
t o t
−
(
E
s u b s
+
E
i n h
)
Where E tot is the overall energy of the entire system, E sub represents the energy of the matrix and a sum of each H2O molecules, and E inh refers to the energy of dissociative JFE.
(16)
E
b i n d i n g
= −
E
i n t e r a c t

The binding energies between Cu (111) surface and inhibitory molecules are 119.6 kcal/mol (quercetin), 119.9 kcal/mol (kaempferol), 120.2 kcal/mol (berberine) and 145.1 kcal/mol (salicifoline), respectively. According to the literature, higher E binding value shows that the inhibitor molecule is more easily and firmly adsorbed at the copper interface. Quercetin, kaempferol, berberine and salicifoline have high E binding, which means that they can be adsorbed at the interface of copper together, thus playing a role in protecting copper. In addition, it is obvious that salicifoline has the highest binding energy, but the results of quantum chemical calculation show that quercetin, kaempferol and berberine have better inhibitory effects than salicifoline, which may be due to the large steric hindrance of methyl group in salicifoline molecules, which makes it difficult for all molecules to be in the same plane, so that they are not adsorbed on the copper interface in parallel, and the density of the adsorption film formed is low, which leads to its corrosion inhibition ability being inferior to the other three molecules [68–70].

Adsorption mechanism of JFE on copper interface is shown in Fig. 13 . Langmuir adsorption model shows that the adsorption of JFE at copper interface includes physical and chemical adsorption. The main components of JFE are quercetin, kaempferol, berberine and salicifoline, which contain a lot of oxygen and nitrogen atoms and can provide lone pair for the empty orbit of copper to form chemical bonds, thus forming chemical adsorption. While the formation of physical adsorption may be due to electrostatic interaction between benzene ring in JFE and copper interface.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Electrochemical impedance data show that JFE has excellent corrosion inhibition effect on copper in 0.5 mol/L sulfuric acid solution. When JFE concentration is 500 mg/L, the η reaches 94.0%, 94.7% and 95.2% at 298 K, 303 K and 308 K respectively.
According to XPS analysis, existence of N-Cu bond shows that there is chemical interaction between the copper surface and JFE.

According to Langmuir adsorption model, JFE is adsorbed on the copper interface by physical/chemical adsorption. SEM and AFM data confirm that JFE has corrosion inhibition effect on copper in sulfuric acid medium.
Through theoretical research, it can be known that quercetin, kaempferol, berberine and salicifoline are adsorbed on the copper interface, which have great influence on the preservative process.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2023R396), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, Municipal Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (no. CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1189 and CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1369), Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST (2022QNRC001), Opening Project of Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (no. 2021CL30), Research Foundation of Chongqing University of Science and Technology (no. ckrc2021052), Opening Project of Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (2022CL18).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2023.105047.
